blocks.text: Verdant Health Advisory LLC
Firm Overview

Verdant Health Advisory LLC is a healthcare operations consultancy. We work for health systems, independent physician groups, and the joint ventures they form together, on the questions that sit underneath the financial statements rather than on top of them: which service lines earn their keep, whether a physician compensation arrangement would survive a fair-market-value review, and where the revenue cycle leaks between the point of service and the posted payment. The firm is small on purpose. The people who sell the work are the people who do the work, and there is no layer between the client and the analysis.
What We Do

Our work is unglamorous and we do not apologize for that. Most of what a health system needs to know about its own operations is already in its data. It is simply spread across systems that were never asked to agree with one another, and nobody has had the time to make them agree. We reconcile those sources, we say plainly where they stop agreeing, and we do not paper over the gap with an assumption the client cannot see. Where an inference is required, we mark it as an inference. That habit slows a report down by a week and has not yet cost us an engagement.

items: Service line contribution analysis: direct and allocated cost by line, with the allocation method stated in full so that it can be argued with rather than merely accepted.
Physician compensation review: arrangement-by-arrangement testing against defensible benchmarks, documented to a standard a fair-market-value review can rely on instead of being asked to trust.
Revenue cycle assessment: registration through posting, tracing where charges are lost, denied, or written off, and separating what is recoverable from what is merely annoying.
Ambulatory and joint venture operations: throughput, block utilization, and the governance questions that follow when two parties own one facility and each has its own report.

blocks.text: Representative Client Work

The firm's current engagement is with {{fact:f:E-2020-001.client}}. Much of this year's fieldwork has been conducted remotely, which has been less of an obstacle than we expected for the data work and rather more of one for the conversations that surround it. We name a client only with permission and we describe an engagement only at the level the client has approved, so this section will always be shorter than a marketing department would like. What we can say generally is that the questions we are asked are rarely new. They are old questions nobody has had time to answer from source, and the answer is usually less dramatic and more actionable than the version circulating in the hallway.
How an Engagement Runs

Richard Marsh manages our engagements: scope, schedule, deliverable dates, and the executed paperwork that has to be in place before fieldwork starts. He confirms commitments in writing the same day they are made, so if you believe you have agreed to something with us, you will have it from him in an email before the day is out. He also plans around the way a client's office actually works. Several of the offices we deal with handle contracting entirely on paper, and their consents and executed statements of work come back to us as scans. We build the calendar around that rather than pretending it away, and we would sooner add a week to the front of an engagement than begin work against an unsigned scope.
Our Standard

Laura Brown, Managing Partner, reads every deliverable before it leaves the office. What she is hunting for is the sentence that claims more than the analysis earned, and she takes it out. Our reports lead with findings, a finding being something the client's own data supports and that we can reproduce from raw inputs. Recommendations follow, in their own section, labeled as recommendations, because a recommendation is judgment and should never be quotable as a finding. We assume general counsel is in the room. In healthcare that assumption is correct often enough that writing any other way is a disservice to the client.

If the question is whether a number is right, we can usually tell you. If the question is whether an arrangement is defensible, we can tell you what the record supports and what it does not, and we will not tell you more than that. A client who wants the second answer regardless of the evidence is better served somewhere else, and we prefer to say so early rather than at the end.
Onboarding Record: Barbara Freeman
Advisory practice. Prepared by Laura Brown, Managing Partner.

Barbara Freeman joins us as an Analyst in the advisory practice. She comes to the firm from the operating side of healthcare rather than from consulting, which is the reason we hired her. Most of what we are asked to analyze was somebody's daily work long before it was anybody's data, and it helps to have a person in the room who has done the daily work. We are a small firm, which means the person who signs the report is the person who read your draft, and nothing you produce disappears into a deck that someone else presents. Barbara, welcome. This record is the version of the conversation you can reread in three months when you have forgotten half of it.

Barbara sits in Advisory and will staff live engagements from her first week. We do not keep a bench and we do not run a training track. You will be given real work on a real client deliverable, and your draft will come back marked up. The markup is the job, not a verdict on the draft. Everyone here has their work marked up, including me. What is being taught is the house standard for what a sentence has to earn before a client reads it.

Three things we will hold you to. First, findings and recommendations stay apart and stay labeled. A finding is what the client's data supports and what you can reproduce from source; everything else is a recommendation and says so on its face. Second, when the client's data runs out, say where it ran out and where you began inferring. You do this instinctively already and you should keep doing it. Third, and this one will feel wrong at first: do not qualify a finding you can support. If the evidence carries it, write it plainly. We will tell you when it does not.

Practical items for your first period. Time is recorded daily against an engagement code, not reconstructed weekly from memory. Client material lives in the engagement folder on the share and nowhere else, your desktop included. Some of our clients work entirely on paper, and their executed documents reach us as scans with nothing searchable inside them, so if you cannot find a signed scope, ask before concluding it does not exist. And ask the naive question in the meeting rather than in the hallway afterward. In my experience the naive question is usually the one nobody in the room could answer, which is precisely why it was worth asking.
Onboarding Record: Joshua Dougherty
Advisory practice. Prepared by Laura Brown, Managing Partner.

Joshua Dougherty starts with us this week as an Analyst in Advisory. He will build most of the models our work rests on, which here is not a support role. The model is the deliverable's spine. The prose around it is an explanation of what the model already proved, and it is worth nothing if the model cannot carry it. Joshua, welcome, and consider yourself warned that you have joined at the point in the year when every client's fiscal calendar closes at once. It is a hard season to start in and a useful one, because you will see in your first eight weeks most of the ways a health system's numbers can disagree with themselves.

The workbooks are Joshua's: pulling the client's sources, reconciling them against one another, and establishing which of two disagreeing reports is the one to believe. That reconciliation is not preliminary to the analysis. It is frequently the whole of it, and it is the part a client still remembers when the same question comes back around. He will also review other people's arithmetic before a deliverable leaves the office, and I expect him to be tiresome about it. The definitional mismatch everyone else read past is the one that ends up in the finding.

The rule Joshua already keeps, stated here for the record: we do not release a figure we cannot reproduce from raw inputs. If someone asks for a number faster than reproduction allows, the answer is no, and that answer is mine to defend rather than yours. Say so and send them to me. A number in one of our reports is a promise that we can rebuild it from source in front of counsel, and that promise is the only thing anyone is actually paying us for. Nobody has ever engaged this firm for a figure they could have guessed.

Practical items. Every workbook carries a methodology tab: source, extract date, and the transformation, in enough detail that a stranger could redo it without asking you anything. Nothing lives on your desktop; the engagement folder on the share is the only copy that exists as far as the firm is concerned. Time goes in daily against the engagement code, against the code and not against a memory of the week. Expect your first drafts back marked up, and let the exhibit carry the argument where it can, but write the caption so that a reader who skips the paragraph still leaves with the point. The office thins out around the holidays, so raise anything that needs a principal sooner than you otherwise would.
Verdant Health Advisory LLC
Firm Overview

Verdant Health Advisory LLC advises health systems, independent physician groups, and their jointly owned facilities on operating questions: which service lines earn their keep, whether a physician compensation arrangement would survive a fair-market-value review, and where the revenue cycle leaks between the point of service and the posted payment. The firm has grown since the last edition of this overview, though not in the direction consultancies usually grow. We have added analysts rather than partners, and we still do not staff an engagement with anyone the client has not met. This edition supersedes the earlier one and describes the practice as it now works rather than as it was described when there was less of it to describe.
Service Lines

The four lines below are not a menu. Most engagements start in one and end up touching a second, because the questions are joined together in the client's operations even where we have separated them on a page.

rows: Is this line paying for itself once allocated cost is honest?
Contribution by line, the allocation method stated in full, and the sensitivity of the answer to that method.
Would this arrangement hold up if someone looked at it carefully?
Arrangement-by-arrangement testing against benchmarks, with the record a fair-market-value review needs rather than a conclusion it is asked to trust.
Where does the money go between the visit and the deposit?
Leakage traced from registration to posting, split into what is recoverable and what is structural.
Two owners, one facility, whose number is right?
Throughput and utilization reconciled to a single definition both parties agreed to in advance.

blocks.text: Representative Client Work

Our first engagement, with {{fact:f:E-2020-001.client}}, set the pattern. The question we were handed looked like a costing exercise and turned out to be a definitional one: two internal reports disagreed and nobody had had the time to find out why. The reconciliation was the work that mattered. The model was the straightforward part and was worth nothing until the definitions underneath it agreed.

{{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} came to us with a question that had a preferred answer already attached to it. We tested it, the record did not support it, and we said so in the finding rather than softening it into a recommendation. The client did not enjoy the report. Their counsel, we were told afterward, did. That engagement remains the clearest illustration we have of why we keep findings and recommendations in separate sections and label them.

{{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}} engaged us this year. The work is underway and we will not characterize findings that are not final. What we will say is that the first weeks again went to establishing which of two internal reports was the one to believe. We have stopped being surprised by that and now budget for it openly in the scope, rather than absorbing it quietly and then explaining why the engagement is late.
How We Write

Laura Brown, Managing Partner, still reads every deliverable before it leaves the office, and what she takes out is any sentence claiming more than the analysis earned. Findings lead. A finding is something the client's own data supports and that we can reproduce from raw inputs, and if we cannot reproduce it, it does not go in. Recommendations follow, in their own section, labeled. We assume general counsel is on the distribution whether or not the cover note says so. We use fair-market-value language only where we mean it and can support it; it is a term of art, not an adjective, and it is worn out fastest by the people who reach for it as decoration.
A Practical Note on Paper

Several of the offices we work with handle contracting entirely on paper: printed, signed, scanned, and returned as images with no text underneath them. Those files are pictures of documents, and nothing we own can search them. We have stopped treating this as a defect to be corrected in the client and started treating it as a fact to be planned around, which in practice means asking for executed paperwork earlier than seems necessary and being pleasant about the third ask. It shapes our schedules more than any other single thing, and a firm that tells you otherwise has not worked in this sector.

We are a small firm and intend to stay one. If your question is whether a number is right, we can usually tell you. If it is whether an arrangement is defensible, we will tell you what the record supports and what it does not, and nothing beyond that.
